name: Parque natural Sabinares del Arlanza - La Yecla — circular desde Lerma
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Lerma, Spain, traverse varied terrain within the Arlanza River Valley. The region features scenic paths, including circular routes through the Parque Natural Sabinares del Arlanza - La Yecla. Cyclists encounter a mix of moderate to challenging sections, with landscapes defined by river shores and rural scenery. The area's elevation changes provide diverse road cycling experiences. Best road cycling routes around Lerma The most popular road cycling route is Parque natural Sabinares del Arlanza - La Yecla — circular desde Lerma, a 80.1 miles (129.0 km) trail that takes 5 hours 27 minutes to complete. This difficult route features over 3,400 feet (1,038 meters) of elevation gain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Río Arlanza from Lerma — circular by Tordómar, a moderate 28.8 miles (46.3 km) path. This route follows the Arlanza River, offering views of the valley and surrounding countryside. Local road cyclists also love the Alto de Peña Alta (1078 m) – La Yecla Gorge loop from Quintanilla del Agua y Tordueles, a 44.5 miles (71.6 km) trail leading through the La Yecla Gorge, often completed in about 3 hours 22 minutes. Road cycling around Lerma is defined by the Arlanza River Valley, the Parque Natural Sabinares del Arlanza - La Yecla, and varied rural landscapes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The routes in Lerma are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 1 review. More than 130 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Lerma's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Lerma, Spain?
A: Road cycling routes around Lerma, Spain, primarily traverse varied terrain within the Arlanza River Valley. You'll find scenic paths, including circular routes through the Parque Natural Sabinares del Arlanza - La Yecla. The landscape is defined by river shores and rural scenery, with elevation changes providing diverse cycling experiences. While most routes are paved, some sections may include unpaved surfaces, adding to the challenge.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easier ride in Lerma?
A: Yes, while some routes are challenging, there are options for various ability levels. For a moderate ride, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1056756602/rio-arlanza-from-lerma-circular-by-tordomar" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Arlanza from Lerma — circular by Tordómar&lt;/a&gt;, which is 28.8 miles (46.3 km) long and follows the Arlanza River, offering pleasant views with manageable elevation.

Q: What are some of the more challenging road cycling routes around Lerma?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1056014657/parque-natural-sabinares-del-arlanza-la-yecla-circular-desde-lerma" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parque natural Sabinares del Arlanza - La Yecla — circular desde Lerma&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult route. It spans 80.1 miles (129.0 km) and features over 3,400 feet (1,038 meters) of elevation gain, typically taking over 5 hours to complete.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in the Lerma area?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes around Lerma are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1056014657/parque-natural-sabinares-del-arlanza-la-yecla-circular-desde-lerma" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parque natural Sabinares del Arlanza - La Yecla — circular desde Lerma&lt;/a&gt; and the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1056756602/rio-arlanza-from-lerma-circular-by-tordomar" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Arlanza from Lerma — circular by Tordómar&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What natural features or scenic views can I expect to see while road cycling in Lerma?
A: Lerma is perched on a hill overlooking the picturesque Arlanza River Valley, providing beautiful views throughout your ride. You'll also cycle through the Parque Natural Sabinares del Arlanza - La Yecla, known for its green foliage and river shores. Don't miss the 'Viewpoint of the arches' (Mirador de los Arcos) in Lerma itself, offering expansive views of the surrounding landscape.

Q: What historical landmarks or attractions can I explore near the road cycling routes in Lerma?
A: Lerma is rich in history. Along or near the routes, you can visit the majestic Ducal Palace, the 17th-century Collegiate Church of San Pedro, and the Arch of La Cárcel, a medieval wall gate. Other historical sites include the Hermitage of La Piedad and various 17th-century monasteries. For specific locations, consider visiting the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5698252" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza Mayor and Ducal Palace of Lerma&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5698240" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collegiate Church of Saint Peter the Apostle&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Lerma, Spain?
A: The region's landscapes, particularly the Parque Natural Sabinares del Arlanza - La Yecla, are especially vibrant during the rainy season, which can offer lush scenery. Generally, spring and autumn provide pleasant temperatures for cycling, avoiding the peak heat of summer or potential chill of winter. Always check local weather forecasts before heading out.

Q: Are there any specific viewpoints or scenic spots recommended for road cyclists in Lerma?
A: Absolutely. The 'Viewpoint of the arches' (Mirador de los Arcos) in Lerma offers stunning panoramic views of the Arlanza River and the surrounding countryside, making it a perfect stop during your ride. The routes themselves, especially those following the Arlanza River, provide continuous scenic beauty.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes around Lerma?
A: The road cycling routes in Lerma are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the scenic beauty of the Arlanza River Valley, and the options available for different skill levels.

Q: Are there options for longer road cycling tours in Lerma?
A: Yes, for those looking for longer rides, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1056014657/parque-natural-sabinares-del-arlanza-la-yecla-circular-desde-lerma" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parque natural Sabinares del Arlanza - La Yecla — circular desde Lerma&lt;/a&gt; is a substantial route at 80.1 miles (129.0 km), offering an extensive tour through the natural park and surrounding areas.

Q: Can I find amenities like cafes or accommodation along the road cycling routes in Lerma?
A: Lerma itself, being a historic town, offers various amenities including cafes, restaurants, and accommodation. The Ducal Palace, for instance, now serves as a Parador de Turismo. While specific cafes directly on every route might vary, you'll find options in Lerma and other towns along the Arlanza River Valley to refuel and rest.
tips: The La Yecla gorge is a deep and narrow gorge excavated in the limestone banks so characteristic of the relief of the Peñas de Cervera, enabled to make a route through a series of footbridges. It is an easy and very attractive walk of a kilometer and a half.

The Santa Lucía pilgrimage is held in Hacinas, Burgos, every year, on the Sunday before San Mateo, which is September 21. It is done in honor of this saint, whose image is believed to have been found in a hole in the rock where the hermitage is currently located.
Right when you pass by the town of Castrovido, there is an old stone bridge that was used in ancient times to cross the river that passes by the town itself.
In the municipality of Hortigüela, on the road to Covarrubias, is the monastery of San Pedro de Arlanza, today in ruins; currently it can be visited.

A very interesting town with perfectly preserved historical heritage. Highly recommended to see the traditional wooden beam houses. The entire town of Covarrubias has been declared an Asset of Cultural Interest since 1965. Of its heritage it is worth highlighting the Torreón de Fernán González, the only Castilian fortress from before the 11th century that is preserved.
